content.text: Why Handoffs and Runbooks Are the Same Problem

On-call handoffs and runbooks are two expressions of the same underlying requirement: that operational knowledge must be explicitly documented and transferred, not held in the head of whoever has been most recently paged. A handoff without runbooks transfers incomplete knowledge — the incoming engineer knows what is happening but not what to do about it. Runbooks without handoffs leave the incoming engineer unable to distinguish a known-and-monitored situation from an active incident they should be responding to.

The handoff procedure is the mechanism for transferring real-time situational awareness. The runbooks are the mechanism for transferring durable procedural knowledge. Both must work, or the on-call rotation degrades into tribal knowledge held by the most experienced engineer.
A runbook that exists only in an engineer's memory is not a runbook — it is a dependency. When that engineer leaves or is asleep, the dependency fails.
Runbook Requirements for Claude Services

Every P0 and P1 alert in a Claude API integration service must have an associated runbook. The runbook must exist before the alert goes live in production — not after the first incident. A runbook written after an incident reflects what one person did in one situation; a runbook written before the alert goes live reflects what the team agreed the correct procedure is.

attrs.alt: Runbook anatomy — six required sections: header, quick triage, immediate actions, diagnosis tree, mitigation steps, and post-incident actions

content.text: The Six Required Sections
Section
Time budget
Purpose
Who reads it first
Header
0 min — always visible
Alert identity, links, owner, last-verified date
On-call (before diving in)
Quick triage
2 min
Confirm alert is real, ongoing, and scoped
On-call (first action)
Immediate actions
5 min
Stop the bleeding; determine if war room needed
On-call
Diagnosis tree
5–15 min
Structured hypothesis testing with concrete check commands
On-call and Tier 2
Mitigation steps
varies
Exact commands to execute for each mitigation path
On-call and Tier 2
Post-incident actions
5 min after resolved
Close incident record; update runbook if steps were wrong
On-call
Header Requirements
The header is the first thing the on-call sees when the alert fires. It must provide all the context needed to orient in under 30 seconds.

# Runbook: ModelServingP99LatencyHigh

**Alert:** ModelServingP99LatencyHigh
**Service:** claude-integration / model-serving
**Severity:** P1 (P0 if burn rate > 14x)
**Last verified:** 2024-04-10 by alice@anthropic.com
**Owner:** @claude-sre-team

**Quick links:**
- [Dashboard: Model Serving Latency](https://grafana.internal/d/model-serving-latency)
- [Alert definition](https://prometheus.internal/alerts?query=ModelServingP99LatencyHigh)
- [Incident channel: #claude-incidents](https://slack.com/claude-incidents)
- [Escalation policy: claude-prod-p1](https://pagerduty.internal/ep/claude-prod-p1)
- [Rollback procedure: #mitigation-rollback](https://this-runbook#rollback)
Diagnosis Tree Format

The diagnosis tree is the most important section. It must be structured as a series of testable hypotheses with concrete commands to validate each one. Vague hypotheses ('check if the model server is unhappy') are useless at 3 AM. Concrete hypotheses with exact commands are actionable.

## 3. Diagnosis Tree

Each hypothesis below includes: the observable symptom that suggests it,
the command to check it, and the action to take if confirmed.

---

### Hypothesis A: Recent deploy caused regression
**Symptom:** Latency spike started within 30 min of a deploy
**Check:**
\`\`\`bash
# List recent deploys in the model-serving namespace
kubectl rollout history deployment/model-serving -n claude-prod
\`\`\`
**If confirmed:** Roll back to previous revision → see §4A (Rollback via kubectl)

---

### Hypothesis B: Memory pressure causing GC pauses
**Symptom:** p99 elevated but p50 normal; model server restarts in the last hour
**Check:**
\`\`\`bash
# Check recent restarts
kubectl get pods -n claude-prod -l app=model-serving | grep -v Running

# Check memory usage
kubectl top pods -n claude-prod -l app=model-serving
\`\`\`
**If confirmed:** If memory > 85% of limit → scale up replicas → see §4B (Scale up model serving)
**If confirmed:** If restart loop → check for OOMKilled → see §4A (Rollback via kubectl)

---

### Hypothesis C: Anthropic upstream API degraded
**Symptom:** Our latency mirrors the Anthropic API latency dashboard
**Check:**
\`\`\`
1. Open https://status.anthropic.com — any active incident?
2. Check our upstream latency panel: [dashboard link #upstream-latency]
\`\`\`
**If confirmed:** This is NOT our service. Update status page that we are monitoring
upstream. No technical action. Notify product owner.

---

### None of the above:
Escalate to Tier 2 (feature engineering on-call). Page via PagerDuty escalation
policy claude-prod-p1. Share this runbook URL and your current findings.
Mitigation Steps Requirements

Mitigation steps must include exact commands with required arguments, any prerequisite access or role assumptions, and the expected output or behavior confirming the action took effect. A mitigation step that says 'roll back the deploy' is insufficient. A step that says the exact kubectl command with the namespace, deployment name, and expected output is usable by any on-call engineer.

# §4A — Rollback via kubectl
# Prerequisites: must have the sre-prod-model-rollback-v2 IAM role assumed
# Verify access: kubectl auth can-i rollout deployment/model-serving -n claude-prod
# Expected behavior: latency returns to baseline within 2–5 minutes of rollback

# Step 1: Check current and previous revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/model-serving -n claude-prod

# Step 2: Roll back to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/model-serving -n claude-prod

# Step 3: Watch rollout progress
kubectl rollout status deployment/model-serving -n claude-prod --watch

# Step 4: Verify latency returning to baseline (watch for 5 min)
# Open Grafana panel: model-serving-latency (link in runbook header)
# Expected: p99 drops below 2s within 3 minutes of rollback complete

# If rollback does not reduce latency within 5 min:
# → Try rolling back one more revision (--to-revision=<N-2>)
# → If still not resolved: escalate to Tier 2 with kubectl rollout history output
Runbook Maintenance Requirements

A runbook that is accurate on the day it is written and wrong six months later is worse than no runbook — it misdirects the on-call. Every runbook must have a verification date, a review schedule, and a clear owner who is responsible for keeping it accurate.
Verification Process

Verification means an engineer followed the runbook as written and confirmed that each command works, each link resolves, and each expected output matches actual behavior. Verification requires access to the production environment — it cannot be done by reading alone.
Trigger
Verification required
Owner
Any deploy that changes the service
Affected runbooks must be re-verified
Feature engineer who owned the deploy
Any IAM or access policy change
Any runbook with access prerequisites re-verified
SRE on the rotation at time of change
Every 90 days regardless
All P0/P1 runbooks re-verified
On-call SRE (part of weekly rotation duties)
Any incident where a runbook step was wrong
Runbook updated immediately after incident
Incident IC
New engineer joins the rotation
They run through at least 3 runbooks in a staging environment
SRE lead or senior rotation member
Runbook as Code

Storing runbooks in a version-controlled repository alongside the service code allows runbook updates to be reviewed as pull requests, linked to the deploys they accompany, and tracked with the same audit history as production code.

# runbooks/ directory structure recommended for Claude services
runbooks/
  claude-integration/
    README.md                          # index of all runbooks and their alerts
    p0-availability-drop.md            # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationCriticalBurn
    p1-latency-high-nonstreaming.md    # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationLatencyBurnP1
    p1-latency-high-streaming.md       # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationStreamingTTFT
    p1-completeness-low.md             # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationCompleteness
    p2-queue-depth-high.md             # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationQueueDepth
    p2-rate-limit-budget-high.md       # maps to: ClaudeIntegrationRateBudget

# Each runbook file has:
# - metadata header (alert name, severity, last-verified, owner)
# - 6 sections as described above
# - linked in PagerDuty alert config via annotation: runbook_url

# PagerDuty alert annotation (in Prometheus alert rules):
# annotations:
#   runbook_url: "https://github.com/org/repo/blob/main/runbooks/claude-integration/p0-availability-drop.md"
Handoff Procedure — Detailed Protocol

The shift handoff is a structured 15-minute synchronous communication at the start of each rotation period. It is not optional, not skippable when 'nothing is happening', and not replaceable by a written Slack message alone. The call ensures that nuance and context that do not fit neatly into a written checklist is communicated.
Handoff Timing

The handoff call should start 15 minutes before the rotation boundary — not at the boundary. Starting at the boundary gives the outgoing engineer no buffer for follow-up questions. A rotation that hands off at 18:00 Sunday should have the handoff call at 17:45.
Handoff Protocol Steps
Outgoing engineer shares their handoff notes document in the on-call Slack channel before the call starts. The incoming engineer reads it before the call begins.
Outgoing engineer verbally confirms the document is current — any last-minute updates since writing it.

Walk through each section: ongoing incidents (status and who is still watching), alert suppressions (what and when they expire), recent changes (last 24 hours), known degraded dependencies, runbook gaps noticed this shift.
Incoming engineer asks questions and confirms understanding — especially for any ongoing incidents.
Outgoing engineer transfers pager ownership in PagerDuty if not automatic.
Both engineers confirm the handoff is complete in the on-call Slack channel.
Handoff Document Template

# On-Call Handoff — Claude Integration Service

## Shift: [outgoing name] → [incoming name]
## Handoff time: [date] [time] [timezone]

---

## Ongoing incidents
<!-- Each active or monitoring-phase incident gets its own row -->
| Incident ID | Severity | Status | Summary | Who is watching | Next action |
|-------------|----------|--------|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| INC-2024-042 | P2 | Monitoring | 503s at ~0.2% — model-serving Wk1 | SRE Alice | Watch for 24h; close if no change |

## Active alert suppressions
<!-- Any alerts currently muted, reduced, or redirected -->
| Alert | Suppressed until | Reason |
|-------|-----------------|--------|
| QueueDepthHigh | 2024-04-22 08:00 PDT | Overnight batch job; expected high queue |

## Notable changes in last 24h
<!-- Deploys, config changes, infrastructure changes -->
| Time | Change | PR/ticket | Status |
|------|--------|-----------|--------|
| Apr 21 14:30 | tokenizer config updated | #2187 | No issues in 28h window |

## Known degraded dependencies
<!-- Upstream services that are currently not at full health -->
None at handoff time.

## Runbook issues found this shift
<!-- Anything inaccurate in a runbook that the incoming engineer should know -->
- Runbook #14 (rollback-tokenizer): IAM role name is outdated. Use `sre-prod-model-rollback-v2`.
  Filed runbook-update-007.

## Recommendations for this shift
<!-- Anything the outgoing engineer would do differently knowing what they know now -->
- Haiku p99 latency is 15% above baseline but within SLO. Watch for continuation — if still elevated on Wednesday, open investigation.
- Monthly budget review is Tuesday — budget state is at 72%, Normal policy.
Runbook Gaps and Alert Coverage Audit

Once per quarter, the on-call SRE lead should run an audit to verify that every active P0/P1 alert has an associated runbook, that every runbook has been verified in the last 90 days, and that every runbook's links and commands still work.
Summary

On-call handoffs and runbooks are both mechanisms for transferring operational knowledge — handoffs transfer real-time situational awareness at rotation boundaries, runbooks transfer durable procedural knowledge for responding to specific alerts. Every P0/P1 alert must have a runbook with six required sections: header (links and identity), quick triage (2 min), immediate actions (5 min), diagnosis tree (hypothesis-driven with concrete commands), mitigation steps (exact commands with prerequisites), and post-incident actions. Runbooks must be verified every 90 days or after any service change — a stale runbook misdirects the on-call. Shift handoffs are 15-minute synchronous calls at rotation boundaries covering ongoing incidents, alert suppressions, recent changes, and degraded dependencies. Runbooks stored in version control alongside service code can be linked to deploy PRs and reviewed like production changes.
Every P0/P1 alert must have a runbook with 6 sections before the alert goes live in production.
Runbook header must include: alert name, severity, last-verified date, owner, dashboard link, incident channel link.
Diagnosis tree: structured hypotheses with exact check commands and per-hypothesis actions — not vague guidance.
Mitigation steps: exact commands with IAM prerequisites and expected output confirming success.
Runbook verification: every 90 days, after any related deploy, and immediately after an incident reveals an inaccuracy.
Shift handoff: 15-min call at rotation boundary covering incidents, suppressions, recent changes, degraded dependencies.
